item.title: Federal PQC deadlines, Scattered Spider plea, and AI agent supply-chain risks
item.content:encoded:
Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London — Should Read

Two alleged Scattered Spider members pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London in 2024, an attack that stole customer data, disrupted services, and caused about £29 million in losses. The case highlights the major operational and financial impact cyber intrusions can have on critical public infrastructure.

  • Two members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London systems in 2024 after previously denying involvement.
  • The intrusion took place between August 31 and September 3, 2024, and disrupted TfL services, including delaying customer refunds after attackers accessed data from the Oyster refunds system.
  • Transport for London said customer data was stolen in the attack, and the National Crime Agency said the incident caused £29 million in financial damage.
  • According to the NCA, the attack forced all 28,000 TfL employees to visit their local offices to reset their passwords.
  • Investigators said they found evidence on devices seized from Flowers’ home, including a screenshot showing connectivity to TfL infrastructure and videos showing Jubair breaching TfL systems.

Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents — Should Read

A fake AI agent skill passed multiple popular security scanners, was added to a trusted marketplace, and reportedly spread to thousands of agents by hiding its real instructions behind an external link that was changed after approval. The key takeaway is that current skill-scanning and trust signals like GitHub stars do not reliably protect AI agents from malicious or mutable external dependencies.

  • Security firm AIR created a fake AI agent skill called brand-landingpage, submitted it to a popular skill marketplace, promoted it with an Instagram ad, and says it reached about 26,000 agents, including some on corporate accounts.
  • AIR reported that every skill security scanner it tested, including Cisco's skill-scanner, NVIDIA's Skillspector, and the scanners used by skills.sh, marked the skill as safe.
  • The skill itself contained no harmful setup steps and instead instructed the agent to install a Stitch SDK by visiting stitch-design.ai, a domain controlled by AIR rather than Google.
  • After the skill had been widely installed, AIR changed the external page so it told the agent to download and run a script that, in the demonstration, only sent the user's email address back to AIR.
  • The article says the core weakness is structural because scanners review a fixed submitted package, while the external web content a skill points to can be changed after approval.

Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution Malware — Should Read

Zscaler uncovered Edgecution, a new malware campaign tied to a Payouts King initial access broker that uses a malicious Microsoft Edge extension and Chrome native messaging to bypass the browser sandbox and run a Python backdoor on victims’ systems. The key takeaway is that attackers are combining Teams-based social engineering with a stealthy, headless-browser extension to gain persistent host access and execute commands while evading many traditional defenses.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed a campaign linked to an initial access broker associated with Payouts King ransomware that deploys a malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution.
  • The campaign begins with social engineering through Microsoft Teams messages and a fake Microsoft update website that delivers setup scripts, an AutoHotKey executable, and an encrypted ZIP archive.
  • Edgecution uses the Chrome native messaging protocol to let a hidden Microsoft Edge extension communicate with a Python-based backdoor that can collect system information, access the filesystem, launch processes, and execute arbitrary code.
  • The setup creates a scheduled task that launches Microsoft Edge in headless mode with the malicious extension loaded, making the extension invisible during normal browser use.
  • ThreatLabz observed Edgecution command-and-control servers communicating over WebSockets on cloudfront.net subdomains hosted on Amazon Web Services.

Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration — Should Read

President Trump signed an executive order requiring U.S. federal agencies to migrate high-value and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography by 2030 for key establishment and 2031 for digital signatures, accelerating the government’s previous timeline. The order also pushes agencies and contractors to inventory where current cryptography is used so they can replace vulnerable algorithms before quantum computers make today’s encrypted data readable.

  • President Trump signed an executive order on June 22 requiring federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography, with deadlines of December 31, 2030 for key establishment and December 31, 2031 for digital signatures.
  • The order says adversaries can collect encrypted U.S. data now and decrypt it later once a large-scale quantum computer exists, a risk described as "harvest now, decrypt later."
  • The executive order accelerates the federal post-quantum cryptography timeline by four to five years compared with the 2035 government-wide target set by the 2022 National Security Memorandum 10.
  • Within 30 days, each agency head must name a post-quantum cryptography migration lead, and within 90 days OMB must issue guidance requiring agencies to review inventories of high-value assets and high-impact systems and submit migration plans.
  • The Federal Acquisition Regulatory Council has 180 days to propose a rule requiring covered contractors to meet NIST's FIPS, including post-quantum cryptography algorithms, by December 31, 2030.

GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns — Should Read

GitHub is updating actions/checkout to block the most common unsafe pull_request_target patterns that let forked pull requests run attacker-controlled code with repository secrets and write privileges. The change is meant to curb "pwn request" supply chain attacks by making these risky checkouts fail by default unless maintainers explicitly opt out.

  • GitHub is updating actions/checkout so that, starting June 18, 2026, it blocks common pwn request patterns by default in pull_request_target and some workflow_run workflows involving forked pull requests.
  • The new behavior refuses unsafe checkouts when a workflow tries to fetch code, refs, or commit SHAs that resolve to a fork pull request, unless maintainers explicitly set allow-unsafe-pr-checkout to true.
  • GitHub said pull_request_target runs in the context of the base repository’s default branch and can expose secrets and a GITHUB_TOKEN with read and write permissions.
  • The risk comes from checking out and running untrusted code from a fork in a privileged workflow, which can let attackers steal tokens or secrets and gain the workflow’s full privileges.
  • GitHub and Socket said the change is only a guardrail for actions/checkout and does not eliminate the need to review any workflow that runs untrusted code with secrets, write access, deployment permissions, or OIDC publishing access.

The Code You Didn't Write Is Still Yours to Defend — May Read

AI agents are creating a fast-growing software supply chain risk by automatically pulling and running unseen packages in sandboxes outside normal security controls. The article argues companies need real-time threat intelligence and ingest-time blocking across builds, laptops, and agent environments to stop malicious dependencies before they run.

  • The article argues that AI agents now pull and run open source packages in temporary environments on users’ behalf, creating software supply chain events outside normal review and scanning processes.
  • It says the threat landscape has changed because AI lowers the skill needed to hide malicious code changes and because more code now runs once inside agent sandboxes without ever reaching a repository.
  • The article states that traditional vulnerability management tools are too slow for current supply chain attacks, which can move from patch visibility or malicious package publication to exploitation or adoption within hours or minutes.
  • It argues that package version cooldown policies reduce one risk but can also delay security patches, widening exposure when patch-to-exploit windows are very short.
  • The author says effective defense requires real-time threat intelligence tied to controls that can block package downloads or builds across build pipelines, developer laptops, and agent environments.

The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You — May Read

This sponsored article argues that AI has shrunk the time from vulnerability disclosure to weaponization to hours, making patching alone too slow to manage risk. Its main takeaway is that organizations should validate whether a CVE is actually exploitable in their environment by testing the required attack-technique chain against existing controls, even when no public exploit exists or live exploitation is unsafe.

  • The article says AI has reduced the time from vulnerability disclosure to weaponization from roughly 53 days two years ago to about 8 hours on average in 2026, according to Zero Day Clock.
  • It states that Verizon's 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report found a median 43-day fix time for known-exploited vulnerabilities, with the share of organizations fully patching them dropping from 38% to 26%.
  • The article argues that automated pentesting cannot cover CVEs that lack a public or safe exploit, systems that are too critical or isolated to test with live exploitation, or day-one vulnerabilities before exploit code is ready.
  • It describes TTP-chain validation as mapping a CVE to the sequence of techniques needed for exploitation and testing each technique against deployed controls to determine whether the exploit chain can succeed without launching a live exploit.
  • As an example, the article uses CVE-2025-29824, a Windows CLFS use-after-free tied to Storm-2460 and RansomEXX activity, and says controls such as allow-listing or LSASS protection can break the required technique chain and make the CVE non-exploitable on a given asset.

Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission — Optional

House leaders unveiled a bipartisan kids online safety bill that advances major restrictions and age-verification rules but drops the controversial duty of care provision many lawmakers wanted to force platforms to redesign harmful features. The omission could complicate Senate support even as the bill gains momentum in the House.

  • The House Energy and Commerce Committee released a bipartisan kids online safety bill that omits the duty of care provision that would have required platforms to take reasonable measures to prevent harms such as suicidal ideation, eating disorders, and cyberbullying.
  • The compromise House bill includes provisions to block minors from using disappearing messages, require AI chatbots to state that they are not human, force porn sites to use age verification technology, regulate video games, and create a data broker registry for platforms receiving kids’ data.
  • Sen. Marsha Blackburn criticized the omission of the duty of care provision and said Big Tech companies will maintain the status quo of putting profit before children's safety without a strong federal standard.
  • The bill would preempt state laws that are weaker than the federal standard while allowing states to enact stricter rules, and it also allows for preemption of some state AI laws.
  • Some digital freedoms experts praised parts of the bill for improving kids' online safety but warned that age verification requirements could threaten privacy and access to constitutionally protected materials.

item.title: LastPass Klue breach, Scattered Spider pleas, and Miasma supply chain fallout
Malicious npm PostCSS Packages and BioShocking AI Browser Guardrail Bypass
WhatsApp phishing campaign abuses fake business docs to install remote access tools
PixelSmash RCE, FortiBleed Credential Harvesting, and ShapedPlugin Backdoor Highlight Supply Chain and AI Agent Threats
PixelSmash FFmpeg RCE (CVE-2026-8461), FortiBleed Credential Sniffer, ShapedPlugin Backdoor, and AI Agent Compromise Vectors
Compromised Argentine Microsoft 365 School Account Phishes U.S. K-12 District
